We can (and should) assign small labels to the 250 elements in ⋃_{e | 250} U_e to minimize the desired sum. Labels outside these 250 elements can be large and do not affect the target sum.

If for some e | f we had m_e > m_f, swapping the maximal labels of U_e and U_f cannot increase any max A_d (it moves the larger maximum to a block that appears in fewer A_d). Hence, in an optimal configuration m_e is nondecreasing along divisibility: e | f ⇒ m_e ≤ m_f. Consequently, for each d ∈ D,
max A_d = m_d,
and the objective becomes
S = ∑_{d | 250} m_d.

All elements of U_e are ≤ m_e, so for any threshold t,
∑_{e ∈ D : m_e ≤ t} φ(e) ≤ t.
Conversely, if these inequalities hold and m_e are distinct integers, one can assign the labels 1,2,…,250 to the blocks to realize those maxima.

To minimize ∑ m_d subject to these, we should take a linear extension (topological order) of the divisibility poset on D and set the m_d equal to the running totals of φ along that order (the smallest feasible values). m_50 = 30+20 = 50,

Think of assigning the integers 1,2,...,250 to the elements of the eight sets S_m. If we define C_m = M_m (the largest label used in S_m), then the feasibility constraint is:
for every t, the total number of elements assigned ≤ t is at most t, i.e.,
∑_{m : C_m ≤ t} φ(m) ≤ t.

Minimizing ∑ M_d = ∑ C_m under the divisibility monotonicity is exactly the problem of scheduling 8 jobs on one machine with:
- processing times p_m = φ(m),
- precedence constraints m → n whenever m | n,
- objective minimize the sum of completion times ∑ C_m.

For this (preemptive model suffices), the optimal rule is to process, among currently available jobs (those whose predecessors are finished), the one with the smallest processing time (SPT rule).

- It is optimal (by an exchange argument on adjacent incomparable jobs) to use the Shortest Processing Time (SPT) rule among available jobs (those whose predecessors are done).

Since for d ∈ D, max A_d = T_d under this order, the sum is

Let D be the set of divisors of 250: D = {1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 125, 250}.

Under this monotonicity, for each d ∈ D we have m_d = M_d (because M_d is at least all M_k with k|d).

We can (and should) use exactly the integers 1, 2, …, 250 within ⋃_{d∈D} S_d to keep maxima small; the other S_k (k ∤ 250) can be placed above 250 and don’t affect the sum.

Thus minimizing ∑ M_d is equivalent to minimizing the sum of completion times in a single-machine scheduling problem with:

The classic swap argument shows an optimal linear extension is obtained by scheduling among available jobs in nondecreasing p_d (SPT) order.

- Then T = S1 + S2 = s1 p2 - p3 = 1·11 - 19 = -8.
- Also, S1 - S2 = -(x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1); hence (S1 - S2)^2 equals the discriminant Δ of f.
- The discriminant of x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 is
Δ = b^2 c^2 - 4ac^3 - 4b^3 d - 27 a^2 d^2 + 18abcd
= 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404.
Thus D^2 = (S1 - S2)^2 = 404.

Hence
P = S1 S2 = (T^2 - D^2)/4 = (64 - 404)/4 = -85,
and
S1^2 + S2^2 = T^2 - 2P = 64 - 2(-85) = 234.

Finally,
J(u) = -u^3 - 8 u^2 + 96 u + [234 + 2 - 2(-8)]
= -u^3 - 8 u^2 + 96 u + 252.

At u = 4,
J(4) = -64 - 128 + 384 + 252 = 444.

Therefore,
(x1^2 - 4x1x2 + x2^2)(x2^2 - 4x2x3 + x3^2)(x3^2 - 4x3x1 + x1^2) = 444.

\boxed{444}

Let p(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 with roots x1, x2, x3. Then
- s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5
- s3 = x1x2x3 = 1

Note
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_i x_j.
For the pair (i, j) with the remaining index k, we have x_i + x_j = s1 - x_k and x_i x_j = s2 - s1 x_k + x_k^2, hence
(x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_i x_j
= (s1 - x_k)^2 - 6(s2 - s1 x_k + x_k^2)
= -5x_k^2 + 4s1 x_k + (s1^2 - 6s2).

With s1 = 1 and s2 = -5 this becomes -5x_k^2 + 4x_k + 31. Therefore
P = ∏_{cyc} (x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2) = ∏_{k=1}^3 q(x_k),
where q(x) = -5x^2 + 4x + 31.

Thus P equals the resultant Res(p, q) = ∏ q(x_k). Compute it via reducing p modulo q:
Divide p(x) by q(x):
p(x) = (-(1/5)x + 1/25) q(x) + r(x), with r(x) = (26/25)x - 56/25.

If β1, β2 are the roots of q, then p(β) = r(β), so
∏_{j=1}^2 p(β_j) = ∏_{j=1}^2 r(β_j) = u^2(β1β2) + uv(β1+β2) + v^2,
where u = 26/25 and v = -56/25, and for q(x) = -5x^2 + 4x + 31,
β1 + β2 = -4/(-5) = 4/5, β1β2 = 31/(-5) = -31/5.

Hence
∏ p(β_j) = (676/625)(-31/5) + (-1456/625)(4/5) + (3136/625)
= -20956/3125 - 5824/3125 + 15680/3125
= -444/125.

Finally, since Res(p, q) = a_q^{deg p} ∏ p(β_j) with a_q = -5 and deg p = 3,
P = Res(p, q) = (-5)^3 (-444/125) = 444.

Therefore, the desired product equals
\boxed{444}.

Let f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 with roots x1, x2, x3. Then
- s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5
- s3 = x1x2x3 = 1

We need P = ∏cyc (x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2) for cyclic pairs (i,j) = (1,2),(2,3),(3,1).

Note r^2 - 4r + 1 = (r - α)(r - α^{-1}) with α = 2 + √3 and α^{-1} = 2 - √3. Hence
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i - α x_j)(x_i - α^{-1} x_j),
so
P = R(α) R(α^{-1}), where R(t) = (x1 - t x2)(x2 - t x3)(x3 - t x1).

Expand:
R(t) = s3 - t P1 + t^2 P2 - t^3 s3,
where
P1 = x1^2 x2 + x2^2 x3 + x3^2 x1,
P2 = x1^2 x3 + x2^2 x1 + x3^2 x2.

Compute P1 + P2 and P2 - P1:
- P1 + P2 = Σ_{i≠j} x_i^2 x_j = s1(x1^2 + x2^2 + x3^2) - (x1^3 + x2^3 + x3^3).
Using Newton’s identities: p2 = 11, p3 = 19, so P1 + P2 = 1·11 - 19 = -8.
- P2 - P1 = (x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1) =: V (an alternating degree-3 identity).

The discriminant Δ of f is
Δ = b^2 c^2 - 4c^3 - 4b^3 d - 27d^2 + 18 b c d for x^3 + b x^2 + c x + d.
Here b = -1, c = -5, d = -1, so
Δ = 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404 = V^2.

Thus
- P1 P2 = ((P1 + P2)^2 - (P2 - P1)^2)/4 = (64 - 404)/4 = -85,
- P1^2 + P2^2 = (P1 + P2)^2 - 2P1P2 = 64 + 170 = 234.

Now set t = α, T = 1/α. With u = t + T, v = t^2 + T^2, w = t^3 + T^3, and tT = 1, one finds
R(t)R(T) = (P1^2 + P2^2) + 2 + (v - u)(P1 + P2) - u P1 P2 - w.

For α = 2 + √3, we have u = 4, v = 14, w = 52. Plugging in:
P = 234 + 2 + 10·(-8) - 4·(-85) - 52
  = 236 - 80 + 340 - 52
  = 444.

Therefore, the desired product is 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 with roots x1, x2, x3. By Vieta:
- S1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- S2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5

Rewrite each factor:
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i + x_j)^2 - 6 x_i x_j.
For the complementary index k, x_i + x_j = S1 - x_k = 1 - x_k, and x_i x_j = S2 - x_k(S1 - x_k) = -5 - x_k + x_k^2.
Hence
(1 - x_k)^2 - 6(-5 - x_k + x_k^2) = 31 + 4x_k - 5x_k^2.

Therefore the desired product is
P = ∏_{k=1}^3 (-5 x_k^2 + 4 x_k + 31) = ∏_{k=1}^3 g(x_k),
where g(x) = -5x^2 + 4x + 31.

Let r, s be the roots of g. Then g(x) = -5(x - r)(x - s), and
P = (-5)^3 ∏_{k=1}^3 (x_k - r)(x_k - s) = (-5)^3 f(r) f(s).

The numbers r, s satisfy 5t^2 - 4t - 31 = 0, i.e., t^2 = (4/5)t + 31/5. Reduce f(t) modulo this:
t^3 = t·t^2 = t·((4/5)t + 31/5) = (171/25)t + (124/25),
t^2 = (4/5)t + 31/5,
so
f(t) = t^3 - t^2 - 5t - 1 ≡ (171/25)t + (124/25) - (4/5)t - 31/5 - 5t - 1
= (26/25)t - 56/25.

Let α = 26/25, β = -56/25. With u = r + s = 4/5 and v = rs = -31/5, we get
f(r)f(s) = (αr + β)(αs + β) = α^2 v + αβ u + β^2
= (676/625)(-31/5) + (-1456/625)(4/5) + (3136/625)
= -444/125.

Thus
P = (-5)^3 · (-444/125) = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 be x1, x2, x3. Set
E = (x1^2 - 4x1x2 + x2^2)(x2^2 - 4x2x3 + x3^2)(x3^2 - 4x3x1 + x1^2).

Note that for a = 2 + √3, b = 2 - √3 (so a + b = 4, ab = 1),
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i - a x_j)(x_i - b x_j).

Thus
E = ∏_{cyc} (x_i - a x_{i+1})(x_i - b x_{i+1}) = P(a) P(b),
where P(t) = ∏_{cyc} (x_i - t x_{i+1}).

Expand
P(t) = x1x2x3 - t(x1^2x2 + x2^2x3 + x3^2x1) + t^2(x1x2^2 + x2x3^2 + x3x1^2) - t^3 x1x2x3.

Let s1 = x1 + x2 + x3, s2 = ∑_{i<j} x_i x_j, s3 = x1 x2 x3. Also set
C1 = x1^2x2 + x2^2x3 + x3^2x1, C2 = x1x2^2 + x2x3^2 + x3x1^2.

Useful identities:
- C1 + C2 = s1 s2 - 3 s3,
- C1 - C2 = -(x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1) =: -V (so V^2 is the discriminant Δ).

For our cubic, s1 = 1, s2 = -5, s3 = 1, hence A := s1 s2 - 3 s3 = -8. Then
C1 = (A - V)/2, C2 = (A + V)/2, and
P(t) = 1 - [(A - V)/2] t + [(A + V)/2] t^2 - t^3.

Since a, b satisfy t^2 - 4t + 1 = 0, reduce:
t^2 = 4t - 1, t^3 = 15t - 4. This yields
P(t) = (1/2)[18 - 54 t + V(5 t - 1)].

Hence
P(a) = (1/2)[(18 - V) + (-54 + 5V)a], and similarly for b. Therefore
E = P(a)P(b) = (1/4)[(18 - V)^2 + 4(18 - V)(-54 + 5V) + (-54 + 5V)^2]
= (1/4)(6V^2 - 648) = (3/2)(V^2 - 108).

Now V^2 is the discriminant of x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1, which is
Δ = a^2 b^2 - 4 b^3 - 4 a^3 c - 27 c^2 + 18 a b c with a = -1, b = -5, c = -1:
Δ = 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404.

Therefore E = (3/2)(404 - 108) = (3/2)·296 = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 be x1, x2, x3. Define
Aij = xi^2 - 4 xi xj + xj^2.

Note that t^2 - 4t + 1 = 0 has roots α = 2 + √3 and β = 2 - √3 = 1/α, and
x^2 - 4xy + y^2 = (x - αy)(x - βy).

Thus
P = A12 A23 A31 = ∏cyc (xi - α xj)(xi - β xj) = F(α) F(β),
where F(t) = (x1 - t x2)(x2 - t x3)(x3 - t x1).

Expanding,
F(t) = (1 - t^3) s3 - t C1 + t^2 C2,
where s3 = x1 x2 x3, C1 = x1^2 x2 + x2^2 x3 + x3^2 x1, C2 = x1^2 x3 + x2^2 x1 + x3^2 x2.

Let S = C1 + C2 and Δ = C2 - C1. Then S = s1 s2 - 3 s3 (with s1 = x1 + x2 + x3, s2 = x1 x2 + x2 x3 + x3 x1), and
Δ = (x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1) (Vandermonde). Hence
F(t) = (1 - t^3)s3 + (t^2 - t) S/2 + (t + t^2) Δ/2.

With β = 1/α, one finds the Δ-linear terms cancel in F(α)F(β), yielding
P = [A0(α)A0(β)] s3^2 + [A0(α)A1(β)+A1(α)A0(β)] s3 S + [A1(α)A1(β)] S^2 + [A2(α)A2(β)] Δ^2,
where A0(t)=1-t^3, A1(t)=(t^2-t)/2, A2(t)=(t+t^2)/2.

Using β=1/α and α^2=4α-1 (so α^3=15α-4), we get the identities:
A0(α)^2 = 50 α^3, A0(α)A1(α) = -5 α^3, A1(α)^2 = (1/2) α^3, A2(α)^2 = (3/2) α^3,
and A0(β) = -A0(α)/α^3, A1(β) = -A1(α)/α^3, A2(β) = A2(α)/α^3.
Hence
P = -50 s3^2 + 10 s3 S - (1/2) S^2 + (3/2) Δ^2.

For x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1, we have s1=1, s2=-5, s3=1. Thus
S = s1 s2 - 3 s3 = -5 - 3 = -8.
The discriminant D = Δ^2 of x^3 + a x^2 + b x + c with a=-1, b=-5, c=-1 is
D = a^2 b^2 - 4 b^3 - 4 a^3 c - 27 c^2 + 18 a b c
  = 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404.

Therefore
P = -50(1)^2 + 10(1)(-8) - (1/2)(-8)^2 + (3/2)(404)
  = -50 - 80 - 32 + 606
  = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 be x1, x2, x3. Denote the target product as
P = (x1^2 - 4x1x2 + x2^2)(x2^2 - 4x2x3 + x3^2)(x3^2 - 4x3x1 + x1^2).

1) Factor each quadratic:
x^2 - 4xy + y^2 = (x - (2+√3)y)(x - (2-√3)y).
Let a = 2 + √3, b = 2 - √3, so ab = 1 and a + b = 4.

Thus
P = ∏cyc (x1 - a x2)(x1 - b x2) = F(a)F(b),
where F(t) = (x1 - t x2)(x2 - t x3)(x3 - t x1).

2) Expand F(t):
Let T = x1^2 x2 + x2^2 x3 + x3^2 x1 and T' = x1 x2^2 + x2 x3^2 + x3 x1^2. Then
F(t) = 1 - t T + t^2 T' - t^3.

Since ab = 1, P = F(a)F(1/a). Set u = a + 1/a = 4. A direct multiplication gives
F(t)F(1/t) = (2 + T^2 + T'^2) + (-(T + T') - TT')(t + t^{-1}) + (T + T')(t^2 + t^{-2}) - (t^3 + t^{-3}).

Thus at u = t + t^{-1} = 4,
t^2 + t^{-2} = u^2 - 2 = 14, t^3 + t^{-3} = u^3 - 3u = 52, and
P = -50 + (T^2 + T'^2) + 10(T + T') - 4 TT'
= (T + T')^2 + 10(T + T') - 6 TT' - 50.

3) Express T + T' and TT' in terms of symmetric sums.
For f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1, the elementary sums are
s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1, s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5, s3 = x1x2x3 = 1.
Power sums: p2 = s1^2 - 2s2 = 11, p3 = s1 p2 - s2 p1 + 3s3 = 19.

- Sum S1 = T + T' = Σ_{i≠j} x_i^2 x_j = s1 p2 - p3 = 11 - 19 = -8.

- For TT', set y1 = x1x2, y2 = x2x3, y3 = x3x1. Then
y1 + y2 + y3 = s2 = -5, y1y2 + y2y3 + y3y1 = s1 s3 = 1, y1y2y3 = s3^2 = 1.
Compute q3 = y1^3 + y2^3 + y3^3 via Newton: q3 = Y1 q2 - Y2 q1 + 3Y3 with q1 = -5, q2 = 23 gives q3 = -107.
Now
TT' = (ab)^3 + (bc)^3 + (ca)^3 + 3(abc)^2 + abc(x1^3 + x2^3 + x3^3)
= q3 + 3 s3^2 + s3 p3 = -107 + 3 + 19 = -85.

4) Final computation:
P = S1^2 + 10 S1 - 6 TT' - 50 = (-8)^2 + 10(-8) - 6(-85) - 50 = 64 - 80 + 510 - 50 = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 with roots x1, x2, x3, and denote s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1, s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5, s3 = x1x2x3 = 1.

Note that for any pair (i,j),
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i - a x_j)(x_i - b x_j),
where a = 2 + √3, b = 2 - √3, and ab = 1, a + b = 4.

Hence the desired product is
P = ∏_cyc (x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2) = ∏_cyc (x_i - a x_j) ∏_cyc (x_i - b x_j).

Factor out x_j and use s3 = 1:
P = ∏_cyc (x_i/x_j - a) ∏_cyc (x_i/x_j - b) = H(a) H(b),
where H(t) = ∏_cyc (x_i/x_{i+1} - t) = t^3 - e1 t^2 + e2 t - 1,
with e1 = x1/x2 + x2/x3 + x3/x1 and e2 = x1/x3 + x2/x1 + x3/x2.

Let
C_forward = x1^2 x2 + x2^2 x3 + x3^2 x1, 
C_backward = x1^2 x3 + x2^2 x1 + x3^2 x2.
Then e2 = C_forward/s3 = C_forward and e1 = C_backward/s3 = C_backward. Also,
C_forward + C_backward = s1 s2 - 3 s3 = -8,
C_forward - C_backward = Δ := (x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1).

Thus e2 = -4 + Δ/2 and e1 = -4 - Δ/2. Therefore
H(t) = t^3 + (4 + Δ/2) t^2 + (-4 + Δ/2) t - 1.

Let A = 4 + Δ/2 and B = -4 + Δ/2. Then
H(t) H(1/t) = - (t^3 + t^{-3}) + (B - A)(t^2 + t^{-2}) + (A + AB - B)(t + t^{-1}) + (A^2 + B^2 + 2).

For t = a, 1/t = b with a + b = 4 and ab = 1, we have
t + t^{-1} = 4, t^2 + t^{-2} = 14, t^3 + t^{-3} = 52,
B - A = -8, A + AB - B = (Δ^2/4) - 8, A^2 + B^2 + 2 = 34 + 2(Δ^2/4).

Hence
P = -52 - 8·14 + (Δ^2/4 - 8)·4 + 34 + 2(Δ^2/4)
  = (3/2)Δ^2 - 162.

Now Δ^2 is the discriminant of f(x) (since Δ = ∏_{i<j} (x_i - x_j) up to sign). For f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1, with coefficients a=1, b=-1, c=-5, d=-1, the discriminant is
Δ^2 = 18abcd - 4b^3 d + b^2 c^2 - 4ac^3 - 27a^2 d^2
     = 18(1)(-1)(-5)(-1) - 4(-1)^3(-1) + (-1)^2(-5)^2 - 4(1)(-5)^3 - 27(1)^2(-1)^2
     = -90 - 4 + 25 + 500 - 27
     = 404.

Therefore,
P = (3/2)·404 - 162 = 606 - 162 = 444.

Final answer: 444.

\boxed{444}

Let x1, x2, x3 be the roots of p(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1. Then
- s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5
- s3 = x1x2x3 = 1

Note that for a pair (i,j) with k the remaining index,
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i + x_j)^2 - 6 x_i x_j
= (s1 - x_k)^2 - 6(s2 - s1 x_k + x_k^2)
= -5 x_k^2 + 4 s1 x_k + (s1^2 - 6 s2).

With s1 = 1, s2 = -5, this becomes
f(x) = -5x^2 + 4x + 31.

Therefore the required product is
P = ∏_{cyc} (x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2) = ∏_{k=1}^3 f(x_k).

Since x_k are the eigenvalues of the companion matrix C of p(x), we have
P = det(f(C)), where
C = [ [0, 0, 1],
      [1, 0, 5],
      [0, 1, 1] ].

Compute:
C^2 = [ [0, 1, 1],
        [0, 5, 6],
        [1, 1, 6] ],
so
f(C) = -5C^2 + 4C + 31I
     = [ [31, -5, -1],
         [ 4,  6, -10],
         [-5, -1,  5] ].

Then
det(f(C)) = 444.

Hence the desired product is 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots be a, b, c of f(x) = x^3 − x^2 − 5x − 1, so
- s1 = a + b + c = 1
- s2 = ab + bc + ca = −5
- s3 = abc = 1.

We are to compute
P = (a^2 − 4ab + b^2)(b^2 − 4bc + c^2)(c^2 − 4ca + a^2).

Note that a^2 − 4ab + b^2 = (a + b)^2 − 6ab, and also
a^2 − 4ab + b^2 = b^2[(a/b)^2 − 4(a/b) + 1].
Therefore
P = (abc)^2 ∏_{cyc} [(a/b)^2 − 4(a/b) + 1] = s3^2 ∏_{i=1}^3 g(r_i),
where r1 = a/b, r2 = b/c, r3 = c/a so r1 r2 r3 = 1, and g(t) = t^2 − 4t + 1.

The numbers r1, r2, r3 are the roots of
Q(t) = t^3 − S1 t^2 + S2 t − 1,
where S1 = a/b + b/c + c/a and S2 = a/c + b/a + c/b.

Since g(t) = (t − α)(t − β) with α = 2 + √3, β = 2 − √3, we have
∏ g(r_i) = Q(α) Q(β).

A direct reduction using α + β = 4 and αβ = 1 yields
Q(α)Q(β) = A^2 + B^2 − 4AB + 10A + 10B − 50,
where A = S1 and B = S2.

Express A, B in terms of the symmetric data of a, b, c. Let
U = ∑_{sym} a^2 b = a^2 b + a^2 c + b^2 a + b^2 c + c^2 a + c^2 b,
and D = (a − b)(b − c)(c − a).
Then the cyclic sums satisfy
T1 = a^2 c + b^2 a + c^2 b = (U − D)/2,
T2 = a^2 b + b^2 c + c^2 a = (U + D)/2,
and hence
A = S1 = T1/s3 = (U − D)/(2s3),  B = S2 = T2/s3 = (U + D)/(2s3).

Substituting into the expression for Q(α)Q(β) gives
Q(α)Q(β) = (−U^2 + 3D^2)/(2s3^2) + 10(U/s3) − 50,
so
P = s3^2 Q(α)Q(β) = (−U^2 + 3D^2)/2 + 10 s3 U − 50 s3^2.

Now use U = s1 s2 − 3 s3 and D^2 = Δ, the discriminant of f. For a monic cubic x^3 − s1 x^2 + s2 x − s3,
Δ = s1^2 s2^2 − 4 s2^3 − 4 s1^3 s3 − 27 s3^2 + 18 s1 s2 s3.

With s1 = 1, s2 = −5, s3 = 1:
- U = (1)(−5) − 3(1) = −8,
- Δ = 1^2·(−5)^2 − 4(−5)^3 − 4·1^3·1 − 27·1^2 + 18·1·(−5)·1
     = 25 + 500 − 4 − 27 − 90 = 404.

Therefore
P = (−(−8)^2 + 3·404)/2 + 10·1·(−8) − 50·1
  = (−64 + 1212)/2 − 80 − 50
  = 574 − 130
  = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let x1, x2, x3 be the roots of P(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1, so s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1, s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5, s3 = x1x2x3 = 1.

Rewrite each factor:
x_i^2 - 4x_ix_j + x_j^2 = (x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_ix_j.

For example, for (i,j) = (1,2), using x1 + x2 = 1 - x3 and x1x2 = s2 - x3(1 - x3) = x3^2 - x3 - 5, we get
(x1 + x2)^2 - 6x1x2 = (1 - x3)^2 - 6(x3^2 - x3 - 5) = -5x3^2 + 4x3 + 31.

Cyclically,
(x1^2 - 4x1x2 + x2^2)(x2^2 - 4x2x3 + x3^2)(x3^2 - 4x3x1 + x1^2)
= ∏_{k=1}^3 f(x_k), where f(t) = -5t^2 + 4t + 31.

Let r1, r2 be the roots of f(t). Then f(t) = a(t - r1)(t - r2) with a = -5. Hence
∏_{k=1}^3 f(x_k) = a^3 P(r1)P(r2), where P(t) = t^3 - t^2 - 5t - 1.

Reduce P(t) modulo f(t). From f(t) = 0, we have t^2 = (4/5)t + 31/5, thus
t^3 = t·t^2 = (4/5)t^2 + (31/5)t = (171/25)t + 124/25.
Therefore
P(t) = t^3 - t^2 - 5t - 1 ≡ (26/25)t - 56/25 (mod f),
so for r a root of f, P(r) = (26r - 56)/25.

Thus
P(r1)P(r2) = [(26r1 - 56)(26r2 - 56)]/25^2
= [26^2 r1r2 - 56·26 (r1 + r2) + 56^2]/25^2.

Using Vieta’s formulas for f: r1 + r2 = -b/a = 4/5, r1r2 = c/a = -31/5. Then
P(r1)P(r2) = [676(−31/5) − 1456(4/5) + 3136]/625 = −444/125.

Finally,
∏_{k=1}^3 f(x_k) = a^3 P(r1)P(r2) = (−5)^3 · (−444/125) = 444.

Therefore, the required value is
\boxed{444}.

Let x1, x2, x3 be the roots of x^3 − x^2 − 5x − 1 = 0. Denote
P = (x1^2 − 4x1x2 + x2^2)(x2^2 − 4x2x3 + x3^2)(x3^2 − 4x3x1 + x1^2).

Rewrite each factor as
x_i^2 − 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = x_j^2[(x_i/x_j)^2 − 4(x_i/x_j) + 1].
Thus
P = (x1x2x3)^2 ∏_{cyc}[(x_i/x_j)^2 − 4(x_i/x_j) + 1].
Since x1x2x3 = 1 (by Vieta), we have
P = ∏_{cyc} f(r_k), where f(t) = t^2 − 4t + 1 and r1 = x1/x2, r2 = x2/x3, r3 = x3/x1 with r1r2r3 = 1.

Let
A = r1r2 + r2r3 + r3r1 = x1/x3 + x2/x1 + x3/x2,
B = r1 + r2 + r3 = x1/x2 + x2/x3 + x3/x1.
Then
∏_{k=1}^3 (r_k − a) = 1 − aA + a^2B − a^3.
Since f(t) has roots α, β with α + β = 4 and αβ = 1, we get
P = [1 − αA + α^2B − α^3][1 − βA + β^2B − β^3].
Reducing modulo t^2 − 4t + 1 gives, for γ = α or β,
1 − Aγ + Bγ^2 − γ^3 = (5 − B) + (−A + 4B − 15)γ.
Hence
P = (5 − B)^2 + 4(5 − B)(−A + 4B − 15) + (−A + 4B − 15)^2
= A^2 + B^2 − 4AB + 10(A + B) − 50.

Now express A + B and AB in terms of the roots. Using x1x2x3 = 1,
A + B = Σ_{i≠j} x_i^2 x_j = (x1 + x2 + x3)(x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1) − 3x1x2x3
= (1)(−5) − 3 = −8.

Let T1 = x1^2x2 + x2^2x3 + x3^2x1 and T2 = x1^2x3 + x2^2x1 + x3^2x2. Then A = T1, B = T2, and
T1 + T2 = −8, T1 − T2 = −(x1 − x2)(x2 − x3)(x3 − x1) =: −D.
Thus AB = (T1T2) = ((T1 + T2)^2 − (T1 − T2)^2)/4 = (64 − D^2)/4.

Therefore
P = A^2 + B^2 − 4AB + 10(A + B) − 50
= (A + B)^2 − 6AB + 10(A + B) − 50
= 64 − 6·(64 − D^2)/4 − 80 − 50
= (3/2)D^2 − 162.

But D^2 = ∏_{i<j} (x_i − x_j)^2 is the discriminant Δ of the cubic (since it is monic). For x^3 + ax^2 + bx + c, 
Δ = a^2b^2 − 4b^3 − 4a^3c − 27c^2 + 18abc.
Here a = −1, b = −5, c = −1, so
Δ = 1·25 − 4(−125) − 4(−1)^3(−1) − 27(1) + 18(−1)(−5)(−1)
= 25 + 500 − 4 − 27 − 90 = 404.

Hence P = (3/2)·404 − 162 = 606 − 162 = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the monic cubic be f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 with roots x1, x2, x3. Then
- x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5
- x1x2x3 = 1

Rewrite each factor using S3 = x1x2x3 = 1:
x1^2 - 4x1x2 + x2^2 = (x1 + x2)^2 - 6x1x2 = (1 - x3)^2 - 6/x3,
and cyclically. Hence
P = ∏_{cyc} [(1 - xk)^2 - 6/xk] = ∏_{k=1}^3 (xk^3 - 2xk^2 + xk - 6)/xk.

Since x1x2x3 = 1, this reduces to
P = ∏_{k=1}^3 (xk^3 - 2xk^2 + xk - 6).

Using the cubic, xk^3 = xk^2 + 5xk + 1, so
xk^3 - 2xk^2 + xk - 6 = -(xk^2 - 6xk + 5) = -(xk - 1)(xk - 5).

Thus
P = (-1)^3 ∏_{k=1}^3 (xk - 1) ∏_{k=1}^3 (xk - 5) = -[∏(xk - 1)][∏(xk - 5)].

But ∏(xk - a) = -f(a), so
∏(xk - 1) = -f(1) = 6, and ∏(xk - 5) = -f(5) = -74.

Therefore P = -(6)(-74) = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let x1, x2, x3 be the roots of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1. By Vieta:
- S1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- S2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5
- S3 = x1x2x3 = 1

We need P = ∏_{i<j} (xi^2 - 4xixj + xj^2). Note
xi^2 - 4xixj + xj^2 = (xi + xj)^2 - 6xixj.

For the pair not involving xk, we have:
(xi + xj) = S1 - xk and xixj = S2 - S1 xk + xk^2. Hence each factor equals
(S1 - xk)^2 - 6(S2 - S1 xk + xk^2) = (S1^2 - 6S2) + 4S1 xk - 5xk^2.

With S1 = 1, S2 = -5, this quadratic is
B(x) = 31 + 4x - 5x^2.

Therefore
P = ∏_{k=1}^3 B(xk) = Res(f, B).

Let α, β be the roots of B(x) = -5x^2 + 4x + 31, so α + β = 4/5 and αβ = -31/5. Since Res(f, B) = (-5)^3 f(α) f(β) (f is monic), compute f(α) f(β) by reducing f modulo B.

Using x^2 ≡ (4/5)x + 31/5 (mod B), we get
x^3 ≡ (171/25)x + 124/25,
so
f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 ≡ (26/25)x - 56/25.

Let R(x) = (26/25)x - 56/25. Then
f(α) f(β) = R(α) R(β) = u^2 αβ + u v (α + β) + v^2,
with u = 26/25, v = -56/25:
- u^2 αβ = (676/625)(-31/5) = -20956/3125
- u v (α + β) = (26/25)(-56/25)(4/5) = -5824/3125
- v^2 = 3136/625 = 15680/3125

Thus f(α) f(β) = (-20956 - 5824 + 15680)/3125 = -11100/3125 = -444/125.

Finally,
P = (-5)^3 f(α) f(β) = -125 × (-444/125) = 444.

\boxed{444}

Let the roots of x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 = 0 be x1, x2, x3. Denote
- s = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- u = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5
- p = x1x2x3 = 1

We need E = ∏cyc (x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2) over pairs (i,j) = (1,2), (2,3), (3,1).

Rewrite each factor:
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_i x_j.
For the pair (i,j) with the missing index k, we have x_i + x_j = s - x_k and x_i x_j = p/x_k. Thus
(x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_i x_j = (s - x_k)^2 - 6(p/x_k).

With s = p = 1, this becomes for each root x:
f(x) = (1 - x)^2 - 6/x = (x^3 - 2x^2 + x - 6)/x.
Using x^3 = x^2 + 5x + 1 (since x is a root), we get
f(x) = (−x^2 + 6x − 5)/x = −(x − 1)(x − 5)/x.

Therefore,
E = ∏_{k=1}^3 f(x_k) = − [∏ (x_k − 1)] [∏ (x_k − 5)] / (x1x2x3)
= − [∏ (x_k − 1)] [∏ (x_k − 5)] (since p = 1).

For the monic cubic f(t) = t^3 − t^2 − 5t − 1 with roots x_k, we have
∏ (x_k − a) = −f(a).
Thus:
∏ (x_k − 1) = −f(1) = −(1 − 1 − 5 − 1) = 6,
∏ (x_k − 5) = −f(5) = −(125 − 25 − 25 − 1) = −74.

Hence,
E = −(6)(−74) = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let x1, x2, x3 be the roots of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1. By Vieta:
- s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5
- s3 = x1x2x3 = 1

Note that for a = 2 + √3 (so a + a^{-1} = 4),
x_i^2 - 4x_ix_j + x_j^2 = (x_i - a x_j)(x_i - a^{-1} x_j).

Therefore the desired product equals
P(a) P(a^{-1}), where P(t) = (x1 - t x2)(x2 - t x3)(x3 - t x1).

Expand P(t):
P(t) = s3(1 - t^3) + (t^2 - t) S1 + (t^2 + t) S2,
where
S1 = (x1^2 x2 + x2^2 x3 + x3^2 x1 - x1 x3^2)/2,
S2 = (x2 x3^2 + x1^2 x3 + x1 x2^2 - x2^2 x1)/2.

More cleanly, set
T = sum_{i≠j} x_i^2 x_j and D = (x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1).
Then S1 + S2 = T/2 and S2 - S1 = D/2, yielding
P(t) = s3(1 - t^3) + (t^2 - t)(T/2) + (t^2 + t)(D/2).

Compute T and D^2:
- Using Newton sums for f(x): p1 = 1, p2 = 11, p3 = 19, hence
  T = s1 p2 - p3 = 11 - 19 = -8.
- The discriminant Δ = D^2 of x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 is
  Δ = c1^2 c2^2 - 4 c2^3 - 4 c1^3 c3 - 27 c3^2 + 18 c1 c2 c3
    = 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404.
  Thus D^2 = 404.

Set A = 1 - t^3, B = t^2 - t, C = t^2 + t. Then
t^3 P(t) P(1/t) = - (A + (T/2) B)^2 + (D^2/4) C^2.

With T = -8, D^2 = 404, this becomes
t^3 P(t) P(1/t) = - (A - 4B)^2 + 101 C^2.

Evaluate at t = a = 2 + √3:
- t^2 = 7 + 4√3, t^3 = 26 + 15√3,
- A = 1 - t^3 = -25 - 15√3,
- B = t^2 - t = 5 + 3√3,
- C = t^2 + t = 9 + 5√3.

Compute:
(A - 4B)^2 = (-45 - 27√3)^2 = 4212 + 2430√3,
101 C^2 = 101(156 + 90√3) = 15756 + 9090√3.

Hence
t^3 P(a) P(1/a) = -(4212 + 2430√3) + (15756 + 9090√3)
                 = 11544 + 6660√3
                 = 444(26 + 15√3) = 444 t^3.

Therefore P(a) P(a^{-1}) = 444, and since the original product equals P(a) P(a^{-1}), the value is 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots be x1, x2, x3 of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1. Note
x_i^2 - 4 x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i - λ x_j)(x_i - μ x_j), where λ, μ are the roots of t^2 - 4t + 1 = 0, i.e., λ = 2 + √3, μ = 2 - √3, with λ + μ = 4 and λμ = 1.

Hence the desired product equals
P = ∏_{cyc} (x1^2 - 4 x1 x2 + x2^2)(x2^2 - 4 x2 x3 + x3^2)(x3^2 - 4 x3 x1 + x1^2)
= ∏_{σ∈{λ,μ}} F(σ), where F(σ) = (x1 - σ x2)(x2 - σ x3)(x3 - σ x1).

Expand F(σ):
F(σ) = x1 x2 x3 (1 - σ^3) - σ ∑_{cyc} x1^2 x2 + (σ + σ^2) ∑_{cyc} x1 x2^2.

Define symmetric sums e1 = x1 + x2 + x3, e2 = x1 x2 + x2 x3 + x3 x1, e3 = x1 x2 x3, and set
E = ∑_{i≠j} x_i^2 x_j = e1 e2 - 3 e3, and A = x1 x2^2 + x2 x3^2 + x3 x1^2.

Then F(σ) = (1 - σ^3) e3 - σ E + (σ + σ^2) A.

Using λ + μ = 4, λμ = 1, λ^3 + μ^3 = 52, and (σ + σ^2)(τ + τ^2) at σ = λ, τ = μ gives:
P = F(λ) F(μ) = 6 A^2 - 6 E A + (E^2 + 10 E - 50).

Next, note the antisymmetric identity
A - (E - A) = x1(x2^2 - x3^2) + x2(x3^2 - x1^2) + x3(x1^2 - x2^2) = (x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1) =: Δ.
Thus A = (E + Δ)/2 and the expression for P simplifies to
P = (3/2) Δ^2 - (1/2) E^2 + 10 E - 50,
which depends only on symmetric invariants.

For f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1, we have e1 = 1, e2 = -5, e3 = 1, hence
E = e1 e2 - 3 e3 = (1)(-5) - 3(1) = -8.

The discriminant Disc(f) = Δ^2 for a monic cubic x^3 + a x^2 + b x + c equals
a^2 b^2 - 4 b^3 - 4 a^3 c - 27 c^2 + 18 a b c.
Here a = -1, b = -5, c = -1, so
Disc = 1·25 - 4(-125) - 4(-1)^3(-1) - 27·1 + 18(-1)(-5)(-1)
= 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404.
Thus Δ^2 = 404.

Finally,
P = (3/2)(404) - (1/2)(64) + 10(-8) - 50
= 606 - 32 - 80 - 50
= 444.

Therefore, the required value is
\boxed{444}.

Let the roots of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 be x1, x2, x3. Then
- s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5
- s3 = x1x2x3 = 1

For each unordered pair {i, j}, with k the remaining index, we have:
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_i x_j
= (s1 - x_k)^2 - 6(s2 - s1 x_k + x_k^2)
= (s1^2 - 6s2) + 4s1 x_k - 5x_k^2.

With s1 = 1, s2 = -5, this becomes:
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = 31 + 4x_k - 5x_k^2.

Therefore the desired product is
P = ∏_{cyc} (x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2) = ∏_{k=1}^3 (31 + 4x_k - 5x_k^2) = ∏_{k=1}^3 g(x_k),
where g(x) = -5x^2 + 4x + 31.

Let β1, β2 be the roots of g. Since g(x) = -5(x - β1)(x - β2),
P = ∏ g(x_k) = (-5)^3 ∏ f(β_j).

Reduce f modulo g using 5β^2 - 4β - 31 = 0:
β^2 = (4β + 31)/5,
β^3 = β·β^2 = (4β^2 + 31β)/5 = (171β + 124)/25,
so
f(β) = β^3 - β^2 - 5β - 1 = (26β - 56)/25.

Thus
f(β1)f(β2) = (1/625)[(26β1 - 56)(26β2 - 56)]
= (1/625)[26^2(β1β2) - 56·26(β1 + β2) + 56^2].

From g, β1 + β2 = 4/5 and β1β2 = -31/5. Hence
f(β1)f(β2) = (1/625)[676·(-31/5) - 1456·(4/5) + 3136]
= -444/125.

Therefore
P = (-5)^3 · (-444/125) = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots be r1, r2, r3 of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1, so
- s1 = r1 + r2 + r3 = 1
- s2 = r1 r2 + r2 r3 + r3 r1 = -5
- s3 = r1 r2 r3 = 1

Factor each quadratic:
ri^2 - 4 ri rj + rj^2 = (ri - α rj)(ri - α^{-1} rj), where α = 2 + √3 and α + α^{-1} = 4.

Hence the desired product is
P = ∏cyc (ri^2 - 4 ri rj + rj^2) = Q(α) Q(α^{-1}),
where Q(t) = (r1 - t r2)(r2 - t r3)(r3 - t r1).

Expand:
Q(t) = s3 - t C1 + t^2 C2 - t^3 s3,
where C1 = r1^2 r2 + r2^2 r3 + r3^2 r1 and C2 = r1 r2^2 + r2 r3^2 + r3 r1^2.

Set T = C1 + C2 and Δ = (r1 - r2)(r2 - r3)(r3 - r1). Then
- T = s1 s2 - 3 s3 = 1·(-5) - 3·1 = -8,
- C1 C2 = (T^2 - Δ^2)/4.

Compute Q(t)Q(1/t). A straightforward combination gives
Q(t)Q(1/t) = (C1 + C2)^2 - 6 C1 C2 + 10 s3 (C1 + C2) - 50 s3^2
when evaluated at t with t + t^{-1} = 4 (and thus t^2 + t^{-2} = 14, t^3 + t^{-3} = 52), i.e. for t = α.

It remains to find Δ^2, which is the discriminant of f(x):
Disc = s1^2 s2^2 - 4 s2^3 - 4 s1^3 s3 - 27 s3^2 + 18 s1 s2 s3
= 1^2·(-5)^2 - 4·(-5)^3 - 4·1^3·1 - 27·1^2 + 18·1·(-5)·1
= 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404.
Thus Δ^2 = 404 and C1 C2 = (T^2 - Δ^2)/4 = (64 - 404)/4 = -85.

Finally,
P = T^2 - 6(C1 C2) + 10 s3 T - 50 s3^2
= (-8)^2 - 6(-85) + 10·1·(-8) - 50·1
= 64 + 510 - 80 - 50
= 444.

\boxed{444}

Let the distinct roots of f(x) = x^3 − x^2 − 5x − 1 be x1, x2, x3. Denote s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1, s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = −5, s3 = x1x2x3 = 1.

Observe for a cyclic pair (xi, xj) with the remaining root xk:
x_i^2 − 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i + x_j)^2 − 6x_i x_j
= (s1 − x_k)^2 − 6(s2 − x_k(s1 − x_k))
= s1^2 − 6s2 + 4s1 x_k − 5x_k^2.

Thus, with s1 = 1 and s2 = −5, each factor equals
−5x_k^2 + 4x_k + 31.

Therefore the product is
P = ∏_{k=1}^3 (−5x_k^2 + 4x_k + 31) = ∏_{k=1}^3 g(x_k),
where g(x) = −5x^2 + 4x + 31.

Using the resultant identity for the monic f, we have
P = Res(f, g) = (−5)^3 ∏ f(y_j),
where y_j are the roots of g, i.e., 5y^2 − 4y − 31 = 0.

Reduce f(y) modulo 5y^2 − 4y − 31 = 0:
y^2 = (4y + 31)/5, y^3 = (171y + 124)/25,
so
f(y) = y^3 − y^2 − 5y − 1 = (171y + 124)/25 − (4y + 31)/5 − 5y − 1
= (26y − 56)/25.

Let y1 + y2 = 4/5 and y1y2 = −31/5. Then
f(y1)f(y2) = [(26y − 56)/25]_{y=y1} · [(26y − 56)/25]_{y=y2}
= (1/25)^2 [26^2(y1y2) + 26(−56)(y1 + y2) + (−56)^2]
= (1/625)[676(−31/5) − 1456(4/5) + 3136]
= (1/625)(−2220) = −2220/625 = −444/125.

Hence
P = (−5)^3 · (−444/125) = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 with roots x1, x2, x3. Then:
- s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5
- s3 = x1x2x3 = 1

We need P = ∏cyc (xi^2 - 4xixj + xj^2) over pairs (1,2), (2,3), (3,1).

Note xi^2 - 4xixj + xj^2 = (xi - k xj)(xi - l xj), where k, l are roots of t^2 - 4t + 1 = 0, i.e. k = 2 + √3, l = 2 - √3.

Define F(t) = (x1 - t x2)(x2 - t x3)(x3 - t x1). Then
P = F(k) F(l).

Expand F(t):
F(t) = x1x2x3(1 - t^3) - t(x1^2x2 + x1x3^2 + x2^2x3) + t^2(x1^2x3 + x1x2^2 + x2x3^2).

Let
S = x1^2x2 + x1x3^2 + x2^2x3,
S' = x1^2x3 + x1x2^2 + x2x3^2,
Δ = (x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1).

We have:
- S + S' = (Σ xi^2)(Σ xi) - Σ xi^3 = p2 p1 - p3 = 11·1 - 19 = -8,
- S - S' = -Δ.

Thus
F(t) = 1 - t^3 - 4t^2 + 4t + (Δ/2)(t^2 + t)
= A(t) + (Δ/2) B(t),
where A(t) = 1 + 4t - 4t^2 - t^3 and B(t) = t^2 + t.

Reduce modulo t^2 - 4t + 1:
- t^2 ≡ 4t - 1, t^3 ≡ 15t - 4,
so A(t) ≡ 9 - 27t and B(t) ≡ -1 + 5t.

Let k + l = 4, kl = 1. Then
- A(k)A(l) = (9 - 27k)(9 - 27l) = 9^2 + 4·9·(-27) + (-27)^2 = -162,
- B(k)B(l) = (-1 + 5k)(-1 + 5l) = 1 - 20 + 25 = 6,
- The mixed term A(k)B(l) + A(l)B(k) = 0 (one can check directly with the linear forms).

Hence
P = F(k)F(l) = A(k)A(l) + (Δ^2/4) B(k)B(l) = -162 + (6/4)Δ^2 = -162 + (3/2)Δ^2.

Compute Δ^2, the discriminant of f(x) = x^3 + ax^2 + bx + c with a = -1, b = -5, c = -1:
Δ^2 = a^2 b^2 - 4 b^3 - 4 a^3 c - 27 c^2 + 18 a b c
= 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404.

Therefore
P = -162 + (3/2)·404 = -162 + 606 = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots of g(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 be x1, x2, x3, with elementary symmetric sums s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1, s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5, s3 = x1x2x3 = 1.

Rewrite each factor:
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_i x_j.
For the cyclic pairs (12), (23), (31), let k be the remaining index; then x_i + x_j = s1 - x_k and x_i x_j = s2 - s1 x_k + x_k^2. Hence
(x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_i x_j
= (s1 - x_k)^2 - 6(s2 - s1 x_k + x_k^2)
= (s1^2 - 6s2) + 4s1 x_k - 5x_k^2
= 31 + 4x_k - 5x_k^2
(using s1 = 1, s2 = -5).

Therefore the desired product is
P = ∏_{k=1}^3 (31 + 4x_k - 5x_k^2).

Let f(t) = 31 + 4t - 5t^2 = -(5t^2 - 4t - 31). If s, s' are the roots of 5t^2 - 4t - 31 = 0, then by the resultant identity,

From 5s^2 - 4s - 31 = 0, we get s^2 = (4s + 31)/5 and s^3 = (171s + 124)/25, so

Thus g(s)g(s') = [(26s - 56)(26s' - 56)]/25^2.

Using s + s' = 4/5 and ss' = -31/5,

= 676(-31/5) - 1456(4/5) + 3136
= -2220.
Hence g(s)g(s') = -2220/625 = -444/125.

Therefore
P = -125 · (-444/125) = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 be x1, x2, x3. Denote s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1, s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5, s3 = x1x2x3 = 1.

Note x_i^2 - 4x_ix_j + x_j^2 = (x_i - α x_j)(x_i - β x_j), where α, β are the roots of r^2 - 4r + 1 = 0, i.e., α = 2 + √3, β = 2 - √3, with α + β = 4 and αβ = 1.

Define Q(t) = (x1 - t x2)(x2 - t x3)(x3 - t x1). A direct expansion gives
Q(t) = (1 - t^3)s3 + 1/2 (t^2 - t)(s1 s2 - 3s3) + 1/2 (t^2 + t) V,
where V = (x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1). With s1 = 1, s2 = -5, s3 = 1, we get
Q(t) = 1 - t^3 - 4t^2 + 4t + (V/2)(t^2 + t).

Hence the desired product is
P = ∏_cyc (x_i^2 - 4x_ix_j + x_j^2) = Q(α) Q(β)
= [A(α) + B(α)V][A(β) + B(β)V],
where A(t) = 1 - t^3 - 4t^2 + 4t and B(t) = (t^2 + t)/2.

Using α^2 = 4α - 1 and α^3 = 15α - 4 (and similarly for β), we find
A(r) = 9 - 27r, B(r) = (5r - 1)/2 for r = α, β.
Then
A(α)A(β) = 81(1 - 3α)(1 - 3β) = 81[1 - 3(α + β) + 9αβ] = 81(1 - 12 + 9) = -162,
B(α)B(β) = ((α^2 + α)/2)((β^2 + β)/2) = ((α + 1)(β + 1))/4 = 6/4 = 3/2,
and the mixed term A(α)B(β) + A(β)B(α) = 0.

Thus P = -162 + (3/2) V^2. But V^2 = ∏_{i<j} (x_i - x_j)^2 is the discriminant Δ of f(x).
For x^3 + ax^2 + bx + c with a = -1, b = -5, c = -1:
Δ = a^2 b^2 - 4 b^3 - 4 a^3 c - 27 c^2 + 18 a b c
= 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404.

Therefore P = -162 + (3/2)·404 = -162 + 606 = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots be x1, x2, x3 of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1. Denote s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1, s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5, s3 = x1x2x3 = 1.

Factor each quadratic:
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i - a x_j)(x_i - b x_j),
where a, b are the roots of t^2 - 4t + 1 = 0, i.e., a = 2 + √3, b = 2 - √3.

Hence the required product is
P = ∏_{cyc} (x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2) = ∏_{t∈{a,b}} ∏_{cyc} (x_i - t x_j).

Let r1 = x1/x2, r2 = x2/x3, r3 = x3/x1. Then
∏_{cyc} (x_i - t x_j) = (x1x2x3) ∏_{k=1}^3 (r_k - t) = ∏_{k=1}^3 (r_k - t),
since s3 = 1. Thus
P = Φ(a) Φ(b),
where Φ(u) = ∏_{k=1}^3 (r_k - u) = u^3 - σ1 u^2 + σ2 u - 1, with
σ1 = r1 + r2 + r3 = x1^2 x3 + x2^2 x1 + x3^2 x2,
σ2 = r1r2 + r2r3 + r3r1 = x1^2 x2 + x2^2 x3 + x3^2 x1.

We need σ1 + σ2 and σ1 - σ2:
- σ1 + σ2 = ∑_{i≠j} x_i^2 x_j = s1(x1^2 + x2^2 + x3^2) - (x1^3 + x2^3 + x3^3).
Using Newton sums for f: x1^2 + x2^2 + x3^2 = s1^2 - 2s2 = 11, and x1^3 + x2^3 + x3^3 = s1(x1^2 + x2^2 + x3^2) - s2 s1 + 3s3 = 11 + 5 + 3 = 19. Hence σ1 + σ2 = 1·11 - 19 = -8.
- σ1 - σ2 = x1^2(x3 - x2) + x2^2(x1 - x3) + x3^2(x2 - x1) = (x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1) =: Δ.
Thus Δ^2 is the discriminant of f. For f(x) = x^3 + ax^2 + bx + c with a = -1, b = -5, c = -1,
Disc = a^2 b^2 - 4 b^3 - 4 a^3 c - 27 c^2 + 18abc = 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404.
Hence Δ^2 = 404.

Now reduce Φ(u) modulo g(u) = u^2 - 4u + 1 (whose roots are a, b):
u^2 ≡ 4u - 1, u^3 = u·u^2 ≡ u(4u - 1) ≡ 15u - 4.
Therefore for t ∈ {a, b},
Φ(t) ≡ (15 - 4σ1 + σ2) t + (σ1 - 5) =: A t + B.

With a + b = 4, ab = 1, we get
P = Φ(a) Φ(b) = (A a + B)(A b + B) = A^2 ab + A B(a + b) + B^2 = A^2 + 4AB + B^2.

Write S = σ1 + σ2 = -8 and Δ = σ1 - σ2. Then σ1 = (S + Δ)/2, σ2 = (S - Δ)/2, so
A = 15 - 4σ1 + σ2 = (54 - 5Δ)/2, B = σ1 - 5 = (Δ - 18)/2.

Thus
P = A^2 + 4AB + B^2 = (3/2)Δ^2 - 162.
With Δ^2 = 404, we obtain
P = (3/2)·404 - 162 = 606 - 162 = 444.

Final answer:
\boxed{444}

Let f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 with roots x1, x2, x3.

1) Rewrite each factor using s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1, s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5:
- For the pair (x1, x2), let p12 = x1 + x2 = 1 - x3 and q12 = x1x2 = s2 - x3p12.
Then
x1^2 - 4x1x2 + x2^2 = (p12)^2 - 6q12 = (1 - x3)^2 - 6(s2 - x3(1 - x3)) = 31 + 4x3 - 5x3^2.

By symmetry,
(x1^2 - 4x1x2 + x2^2)(x2^2 - 4x2x3 + x3^2)(x3^2 - 4x3x1 + x1^2)
= ∏_{i=1}^3 (31 + 4xi - 5xi^2).

Let g(x) = -5x^2 + 4x + 31. Then the desired product is E = ∏ g(xi).

2) Let β1, β2 be the roots of 5x^2 - 4x - 31 = 0 (so g(x) = -5(x - β1)(x - β2)). Since f is monic,
E = ∏ g(xi) = (-5)^3 ∏ (xi - β1) ∏ (xi - β2) = (-5)^3 f(β1) f(β2).

3) Reduce f modulo the quadratic q(x) = 5x^2 - 4x - 31:
From q(x), x^2 = (4x + 31)/5 and x^3 = (171x + 124)/25.
Thus
f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 ≡ (171x + 124)/25 - (4x + 31)/5 - 5x - 1
= (26x - 56)/25 =: r(x).

Hence f(βj) = r(βj) for j = 1,2. Writing r(x) = u x + v with u = 26/25, v = -56/25 and using β1 + β2 = 4/5, β1β2 = -31/5, we get
f(β1) f(β2) = r(β1) r(β2) = (uβ1 + v)(uβ2 + v) = u^2(β1β2) + uv(β1 + β2) + v^2
= (676/625)(-31/5) + (-1456/625)(4/5) + (3136/625)
= -444/125.

4) Therefore
E = (-5)^3 · ( -444/125 ) = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots of f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 be x1, x2, x3. Denote the elementary symmetric sums:
- s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1
- s2 = x1 x2 + x2 x3 + x3 x1 = -5
- s3 = x1 x2 x3 = 1

We need P = ∏cyc (xi^2 - 4 xi xj + xj^2), with cyclic pairs (x1,x2), (x2,x3), (x3,x1).

Note that a^2 - 4ab + b^2 = (a - r b)(a - s b), where r,s are the roots of t^2 - 4t + 1 = 0, i.e. r = 2 + √3, s = 2 - √3, with r + s = 4, rs = 1. Hence
P = F_r F_s, where F_t = (x1 - t x2)(x2 - t x3)(x3 - t x1).

Expanding,
F_t = (1 - t^3) s3 + t^2 B - t A,
where
A = x1^2 x2 + x2^2 x3 + x3^2 x1, and B = x1^2 x3 + x2^2 x1 + x3^2 x2.

Let S = A + B and Δ = (x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1). Then A - B = -Δ, so
A = (S - Δ)/2, B = (S + Δ)/2.

Compute S using Newton sums. Let p2 = x1^2 + x2^2 + x3^2 and p3 = x1^3 + x2^3 + x3^3. From s1=1, s2=-5, s3=1:
- p2 = s1^2 - 2 s2 = 1 - 2(-5) = 11
- p3 = s1 p2 - s2 p1 + 3 s3 = 1·11 - (-5)·1 + 3·1 = 19

Then S = A + B = ∑ x_i^2(x_{i+1} + x_{i+2}) = s1 p2 - p3 = 11 - 19 = -8.

The discriminant Δ^2 = ∏_{i<j} (xi - xj)^2 of the cubic x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 equals
a^2 b^2 - 4 b^3 - 4 a^3 c - 27 c^2 + 18 a b c
for a = -1, b = -5, c = -1, which yields 404. Thus Δ^2 = 404.

Now write
F_r = α + C S + D Δ, with α = 1 - r^3, C = (r^2 - r)/2, D = (r^2 + r)/2,
and
F_s = β + C' S + D' Δ, with β = 1 - s^3, C' = (s^2 - s)/2, D' = (s^2 + s)/2.

Using r + s = 4, rs = 1, one checks:
- αβ = (1 - r^3)(1 - s^3) = 2 - (r^3 + s^3) = 2 - [(r+s)^3 - 3rs(r+s)] = 2 - (64 - 12) = -50
- α C' + β C = 10
- C C' = -1/2
- D D' = 3/2
- α D' + β D = 0 and C D' + D C' = 0 (so all linear-in-Δ terms cancel)

Therefore
P = F_r F_s = αβ + (α C' + β C) S + (C C') S^2 + (D D') Δ^2
= (-50) + 10(-8) + (-1/2)(64) + (3/2)(404)
= -50 - 80 - 32 + 606
= 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the roots be x1, x2, x3, with elementary symmetric sums:

- s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5
- s3 = x1x2x3 = 1

Rewrite each factor:
x_i^2 - 4x_ix_j + x_j^2 = (x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_ix_j.

For the pair (i,j), let k be the remaining index. Then x_i + x_j = s1 - x_k and x_ix_j = s2 - s1x_k + x_k^2. Hence
(x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_ix_j = (1 - x_k)^2 - 6(-5 - x_k + x_k^2) = -5x_k^2 + 4x_k + 31.

Therefore the desired product is
P = ∏_{k=1}^3 (-5x_k^2 + 4x_k + 31) = y1 y2 y3,
where y_k = -5x_k^2 + 4x_k + 31.

Compute power sums of x:
p0 = 3, p1 = 1, p2 = 11.
Using the recurrence p_n = s1 p_{n-1} - s2 p_{n-2} + s3 p_{n-3} = p_{n-1} + 5p_{n-2} + p_{n-3}, we get
p3 = 19, p4 = 75, p5 = 181, p6 = 575.

Let a = -5, b = 4, c = 31. Then
S1 = Σ y_k = a p2 + b p1 + 3c = -5·11 + 4·1 + 93 = 42.
S2 = Σ_{i<j} y_i y_j = 376 (computed via symmetric sums; consistency check below).
Compute q2 = Σ y_k^2:
q2 = a^2 p4 + 2ab p3 + (b^2 + 2ac) p2 + 2bc p1 + 3c^2
= 25·75 - 40·19 - 294·11 + 248·1 + 2883 = 1012.
This satisfies q2 = S1^2 - 2S2 ⇒ 1012 = 42^2 - 2S2, yielding S2 = 376.

Compute q3 = Σ y_k^3:
q3 = a^3 p6 + 3a^2 b p5 + 3(a^2 c + a b^2) p4 + (6abc + b^3) p3
      + 3(a c^2 + b^2 c) p2 + 3 b c^2 p1 + 3 c^3
= -125·575 + 300·181 + 2085·75 - 3656·19 - 12927·11 + 11532·1 + 89373
= 28044.

By Newton’s identity for three variables:
S3 = y1 y2 y3 = (q3 - S1 q2 + S2 S1)/3 = (28044 - 42·1012 + 376·42)/3 = 444.

Therefore, the required product is
\boxed{444}.

Let f(x) = x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 with roots x1, x2, x3. Then s1 = x1 + x2 + x3 = 1, s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1 = -5, s3 = x1x2x3 = 1.

Rewrite each factor:
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i + x_j)^2 - 6x_i x_j.

Cyclically, this becomes:
P = ∏_{cyc} [(s1 - x_k)^2 - 6(s3/x_k)] = ∏_{k=1}^3 [((1 - x_k)^2 x_k - 6)/x_k].

Thus
P = (∏_{k} H(x_k)) / (x1 x2 x3) where H(x) = x(1 - x)^2 - 6 = x^3 - 2x^2 + x - 6.
Since s3 = x1 x2 x3 = 1, we get P = ∏_{k} H(x_k).

Because f is monic, ∏_{k} H(x_k) equals the resultant Res(f, H). Compute it via remainders:
H - f = -x^2 + 6x - 5 = -s(x), where s(x) = x^2 - 6x + 5.
Hence Res(f, H) = Res(f, -s) = -Res(f, s).

Divide f by s:
f(x) = (x)(s(x)) + (5x^2 - 10x - 1),
and then (5x^2 - 10x - 1) = 5 s(x) + (20x - 26).
Thus the remainder t is 20x - 26, and with lc(s) = 1,
Res(f, s) = Res(s, t).

For t(x) = 20x - 26, whose root is 13/10, we have
Res(s, t) = 20^{deg s} s(13/10) = 20^2 [(13/10)^2 - 6(13/10) + 5]
= 400*(-111/100) = -444.

Therefore Res(f, H) = -Res(f, s) = 444, and hence P = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let s1 = x1 + x2 + x3, s2 = x1x2 + x2x3 + x3x1, s3 = x1x2x3. From x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 = 0, we have
- s1 = 1, s2 = -5, s3 = 1.

Note
x_i^2 - 4x_i x_j + x_j^2 = (x_i - a x_j)(x_i - b x_j),
where a = 2 + √3, b = 2 - √3 (so a + b = 4, ab = 1).

Define Q(t) = (x1 - t x2)(x2 - t x3)(x3 - t x1). Then the desired product is
P = Q(a) Q(b).

Expand:
Q(t) = (1 - t^3)s3 - t S1 + t^2 S2,
where S1 = x1^2x2 + x2^2x3 + x3^2x1 and S2 = x1x2^2 + x2x3^2 + x3x1^2.

Compute:
- Using Newton sums, p2 = x1^2 + x2^2 + x3^2 = s1^2 - 2s2 = 11, and p3 = x1^3 + x2^3 + x3^3 = s1 p2 - s2 s1 + 3s3 = 19.
- Hence S1 + S2 = s1 p2 - p3 = -8.
- Also S1 - S2 = -Δ', where Δ' = (x1 - x2)(x2 - x3)(x3 - x1). Thus (S1 - S2)^2 = Δ'^2 = Disc(f).

Let U = S1 + S2 = -8, V = S1 - S2 = -Δ'. Then
Q(t) = (1 - t^3) + (U/2)(t^2 - t) - (V/2)(t + t^2)
= 1 + 4t - 4t^2 - t^3 + (Δ'/2)(t + t^2).

For t satisfying t^2 - 4t + 1 = 0 (i.e. t = a or b), reduce:
- t^2 = 4t - 1, t^3 = 15t - 4,
- Hence A(t) = 1 + 4t - 4t^2 - t^3 = 9 - 27t, and B(t) = t + t^2 = 5t - 1,
so Q(t) = A(t) + (Δ'/2) B(t).

Therefore,
P = Q(a)Q(b) = [A(a) + (Δ'/2)B(a)][A(b) + (Δ'/2)B(b)]
= A(a)A(b) + (Δ'^2/4) B(a)B(b),
since the mixed term vanishes (using a + b = 4, ab = 1).

Compute:
- A(a)A(b) = (9 - 27a)(9 - 27b) = 81[1 - 3(a + b) + 9ab] = 81(1 - 12 + 9) = -162.
- B(a)B(b) = (5a - 1)(5b - 1) = 25ab - 5(a + b) + 1 = 25 - 20 + 1 = 6.

The discriminant of x^3 - x^2 - 5x - 1 is
Disc = A^2B^2 - 4B^3 - 4A^3C - 27C^2 + 18ABC
with A = -1, B = -5, C = -1, yielding Disc = 404 = Δ'^2.

Thus
P = -162 + (404/4)·6 = -162 + 606 = 444.

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the sequence of top faces on the six rolls be X1, X2, ..., X6, each uniformly random in {1,2,3,4,5,6}, independent.

Even stickers visible means:
- Sticker 2 is not covered later: X2 ∉ {X3, X4, X5, X6}.
- Sticker 4 is not covered later: X4 ∉ {X5, X6}.
Sticker 6 is always visible.

Thus, with S = {1,...,6} \ {X2}, we have under this condition:
- X3, X4 are i.i.d. uniform on S (|S|=5).
- Given X4 = b ∈ S, X5 and X6 are i.i.d. uniform on S \ {b} (size 4), independent of X3.
Also, X1 is independent of this conditioning and uniform on {1,...,6}.

The probability of this “even-visible” event E is:
P(E) = P(X3, X4, X5, X6 ≠ X2) · P(X5, X6 ≠ X4 | previous)
= (5/6)^4 · (4/5)^2 = 25/81.

Exactly one face left blank is equivalent to exactly five distinct faces among X1,...,X6, i.e., exactly one pair occurs and all other values are distinct.

Under E, the only possible equal pairs are:
(1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (1,5), (1,6), (3,4), (3,5), (3,6), (5,6).
For each of these nine cases, a direct calculation under the conditional distribution shows the probability is 1/20. For example:
- Pair (1,2): need X1 = X2 and X3, X4, X5, X6 all distinct (in S with X5, X6 ≠ X4). This has probability (1/6) · (4/5) · (3/4) · (1/2) = 1/20.
- Pair (3,4): need X3 = X4, X5 ≠ X6, and X1 different from {X2, X3, X5, X6}, which yields (1/5) · (3/4) · (1/3) = 1/20.
- Pair (5,6): need X3 ≠ X4, X5 = X6 ≠ X3, and X1 avoiding {X2, X3, X4, X5}, giving (4/5) · (3/16) · (1/3) = 1/20.
Similar calculations (or symmetry) give 1/20 for the other six types.

Therefore,
P(exactly one pair | E) = 9 · (1/20) = 9/20.

Thus p = 9/20, so m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

Final answer: \boxed{29}

Step 1: Reformulate the process
At the end, a sticker k is visible if and only if the face rolled at time k is not rolled again later (i.e., time k is the last occurrence of that face). Therefore, the visible stickers correspond exactly to the last occurrences (from the right) of the faces rolled.

A face is blank at the end if it was never rolled. Hence, “exactly one face left blank” is equivalent to “exactly 5 distinct faces are rolled in the 6 rolls.”

The condition “all even-numbered stickers are visible” means:
- s2 does not appear in positions 3–6.
- s4 does not appear in positions 5–6.
- s6 is always visible (no restriction beyond that).

Step 2: Count total sequences under the condition (denominator N)
- s1: 6 choices (no restriction).
- s2: 6 choices.
- s3: 5 choices (cannot be s2).
- s4: 5 choices (cannot be s2; may equal s3).
- s5: 4 choices (cannot be s2 or s4).
- s6: 4 choices (cannot be s2 or s4).

Thus N = 6·6·5·5·4·4 = 14400.

Step 3: Count sequences with exactly 5 distinct faces under the condition (numerator N5)
Exactly 5 distinct faces means exactly one pair of positions has the same face, and all others are distinct.

The pair of equal positions must respect the visibility constraints:
- Position 2 can only pair with 1 (cannot pair with 3–6).
- Position 4 cannot pair with 5 or 6 (but can pair with 1 or 3).
All other pairs are allowed.

Total unordered pairs = C(6,2) = 15. Forbidden pairs are {2,3}, {2,4}, {2,5}, {2,6}, {4,5}, {4,6} (6 pairs). Hence allowed pairs = 15 − 6 = 9.

For each allowed pair:
- Choose the duplicated face: 6 ways.
- Assign distinct faces to the remaining 4 positions from the remaining 5 faces: P(5,4) = 5·4·3·2 = 120 ways.

Thus N5 = 9 · 6 · 120 = 6480.

Step 4: Compute the conditional probability
p = N5 / N = 6480 / 14400 = 9/20.

Therefore, m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

Final answer: \boxed{29}

Let X1, X2, ..., X6 be the sequence of top faces on the six rolls.

- A sticker t is visible at the end iff the face at time t does not appear again at any later time.

  - X4 ≠ X5, X6
  - (X6 is always visible)

Let C be this event. Then
P(C) = P(X3..X6 all ≠ X2) · P(X5, X6 both ≠ X4 | X3..X6 ≠ X2)
= (5/6)^4 · (4/5)^2 = 25/81.

We want P(S=5 | C), where S=5 means exactly one face is left blank, i.e., exactly one value appears twice and the other four are distinct.

In sequences with exactly one pair, the pair’s positions (i < j) can be any of the 15 unordered pairs. Under C, the pair cannot be:
- (2, j) for j = 3,4,5,6 (since X2 cannot match any later Xi),
- (4, 5) or (4, 6) (since X4 cannot match X5 or X6).

Thus, among the 15 pairs, 6 are forbidden, leaving 9 allowed pairs.

For any fixed pair (i, j), the number of sequences with exactly one pair at (i, j) is:
6 choices for the paired value × permutations of 4 distinct values from the remaining 5 for the other 4 positions = 6 × 5P4 = 6 × 120 = 720.
Hence the total number of one-pair sequences is 15 × 720, and those satisfying C are 9 × 720. Therefore
P(S=5 ∩ C) = (9 × 720)/6^6 = 5/36.

Finally,
p = P(S=5 | C) = (5/36) / (25/81) = 9/20.

Thus m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

In particular, X4 ≠ X2 and X2 ≠ any of X3, X4, X5, X6.

Now count sequences under E. Let a = X2 and b = X4 (with a ≠ b). Under E:
- X1 ∈ [6] (6 choices),
- X3 ∈ [6]\{a} (5 choices),
- X5, X6 ∈ [6]\{a, b} (4 choices each).
So for fixed (a, b), there are 6·5·4·4 = 480 sequences. Over 6·5 = 30 ordered pairs (a, b), total sequences satisfying E are 14400, consistent with P(E) = 14400/6^6 = 25/81.

We need, under E, exactly one blank face ⇔ exactly one duplicate among the six positions. For fixed (a, b), count (X1, X3, X5, X6) leading to exactly one duplicate. This duplicate can occur in the following mutually exclusive ways:

- (1,2) with value a: X1 = a, X3 ∈ [6]\{a, b} (4 ways), and X5, X6 are distinct elements of [6]\{a, b, X3} (3·2 = 6 ways). Total 24.
- (1,4) with value b: similarly 24.
- (3,4) with value b: similarly 24.
- A pair among {1,3,5,6} with a value c ∈ [6]\{a, b}, all others distinct: choose c (4 ways), choose which pair among the 6 possible pairs (6 ways), and assign distinct values from the remaining 3 choices to the other two positions (3·2 = 6 ways). Total 4·6·6 = 144.

Thus per (a, b), the count is 24 + 24 + 24 + 144 = 216. Across 30 ordered (a, b), total favorable sequences are 30·216 = 6480.

- F3: any of 5 (≠ a);
- F5, F6: any of 4 each (≠ a,b).
So for fixed (a,b) there are 6·5·4·4 = 480 sequences. There are 6·5 choices of (a,b), so total sequences satisfying E is 30·480 = 14400 (consistent with P(E)=25/81).

Now count, for fixed (a,b), how many of these have exactly 5 distinct faces (i.e., exactly one repeated face overall). The repeated face can be:

1) a (times 1 and 2). Then:
- F1 = a (1 way),
- F3 ≠ a,b (4 ways),
- F5, F6 are distinct and ≠ a,b,F3 (P(3,2)=6 ways).
Total: 1·4·6 = 24.

2) b (times j and 4 with j ∈ {1,3}). Two subcases:
- F3 = b, F1 ∈ {6}\{a,b} (4 ways), and F5, F6 distinct in the remaining 3 values (6 ways): 4·6 = 24.
- F1 = b, F3 ∈ {6}\{a,b} (4 ways), and F5, F6 as above (6 ways): 4·6 = 24.
Total: 24+24 = 48.

3) Some c ∉ {a,b}, repeated among positions {1,3,5,6}. Then all of F1, F3, F5, F6 lie in S0 = {6}\{a,b} (size 4), and the 4-tuple has pattern [2,1,1]:
- choose c (4 ways),
- choose the pair positions among 4 (C(4,2)=6),
- choose and order the remaining two distinct values from the other 3 (P(3,2)=6).
Total: 4·6·6 = 144.

Sum for fixed (a,b): 24+48+144 = 216. Therefore the conditional probability is
p = 216/480 = 9/20.

Thus m+n = 9+20 = 29.

Think of the process as six independent die rolls X1, X2, ..., X6. A face ends with the visible sticker t if and only if t is the last time that face appears among the six rolls. Thus the visible stickers are exactly the indices that are last occurrences in the sequence.

Define Nt = 1 if t is a last occurrence (so sticker t is visible), and 0 otherwise. Scanning backward from t = 6 to 1, let kt be the number of distinct faces seen among positions t+1..6. Then:
- P(Nt = 1 | kt = k) = (6 - k)/6,
- and if Nt = 1, then kt-1 = k + 1; else kt-1 = k.

We are given E: N2 = N4 = N6 = 1 (all even stickers are visible). The event A = “exactly one face left blank” means exactly five distinct faces appeared, i.e., exactly five last occurrences. Since 2, 4, 6 are already last occurrences, A is equivalent to exactly two of N1, N3, N5 being 1.

Work backward:

- At t = 6: N6 = 1 always, so k5 = 1.

- At t = 5: P(N5 = 1) = 5/6, else 1/6. Then k4 = 2 if N5 = 1, else 1.

- At t = 4: Condition on N4 = 1. We have
  P(N4 = 1 | k4 = 2) = 4/6, and P(N4 = 1 | k4 = 1) = 5/6.
  By Bayes, P(N5 = 1 | N4 = 1) = 4/5, P(N5 = 0 | N4 = 1) = 1/5.
  Correspondingly k3 = 3 if N5 = 1, and k3 = 2 if N5 = 0.

- At t = 3: If k3 = 3 then P(N3 = 1) = 1/2; if k3 = 2 then P(N3 = 1) = 2/3.
  Now also impose N2 = 1 at t = 2. Using Bayes to condition on N2 = 1, we get the joint posterior on (N5, N3):
  - P(N5 = 1, N3 = 1 | E) = 3/10,
  - P(N5 = 1, N3 = 0 | E) = 9/20,
  - P(N5 = 0, N3 = 1 | E) = 3/20,
  - P(N5 = 0, N3 = 0 | E) = 1/10.

- At t = 1: Compute k1 (the number of distinct faces among positions 2..6) per branch:
  - If (N5, N3) = (1,1): k1 = 5, so P(N1 = 1) = 1/6.
  - If (1,0) or (0,1): k1 = 4, so P(N1 = 1) = 1/3.
  - If (0,0): k1 = 3, so P(N1 = 1) = 1/2.

Now compute the probability that exactly two of N5, N3, N1 are 1 under E:
- From (1,1): need N1 = 0 → contribution = (3/10)·(5/6) = 1/4.
- From (1,0): need N1 = 1 → contribution = (9/20)·(1/3) = 3/20.
- From (0,1): need N1 = 1 → contribution = (3/20)·(1/3) = 1/20.
- From (0,0): impossible to reach sum 2 → contribution = 0.

Total p = 1/4 + 3/20 + 1/20 = 9/20.

Thus m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

Final answer: \boxed{29}

Let X1, X2, ..., X6 be the sequence of top faces when rolling the fair die (faces labeled 1–6). A face is blank at the end iff it never appears among the Xi. The visible stickers are exactly those times t for which Xt does not appear again later; i.e., t is the last occurrence time of its face.

Define:
- A: all even-numbered stickers (2,4,6) are visible. This means X2 ≠ X3, X4, X5, X6 and X4 ≠ X5, X6 (X6 is always last).
- B: exactly one face is blank ⇔ exactly five distinct faces appear among X1..X6 ⇔ exactly one pair of equal values and the other four are distinct.

Compute P(A):
- Choose X2 arbitrarily: 6 ways.
- Choose X3..X6 all ≠ X2: 5^4 ways.
- Additionally require X4 ≠ X5 and X4 ≠ X6: for X4 (5 choices), X5 (4 choices), X6 (4 choices).
- X1 is free: 6 ways.
Thus count = 6·(5·5·4·4)·6 = 14400, so P(A) = 14400 / 6^6 = 25/81.

Compute P(A ∩ B) via last-occurrence positions:
Under B there is exactly one non-last position r (the earlier index of the duplicated face); all other positions are last occurrences. For A to hold, positions 2,4,6 must be last, so r ∈ {1,3,5}. For a random sequence with exactly one duplicate, the earlier index r has distribution P(r) = (6−r)/15 over r = 1..5. Hence
P(A | B) = P(r ∈ {1,3,5}) = (5+3+1)/15 = 3/5.

Unconditionally, the number of sequences with exactly one duplicate is:
6 choices for the duplicated face, C(6,2) choices for its positions, choose 4 of the remaining 5 faces and permute among the other 4 positions: 6·15·5·4! = 10800. Thus
P(B) = 10800 / 6^6 = 25/108.

Therefore P(A ∩ B) = P(B)·P(A | B) = (25/108)·(3/5) = 5/36.

Finally, the desired conditional probability is
p = P(B | A) = P(A ∩ B) / P(A) = (5/36) / (25/81) = 9/20.

Thus m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

Final answer: \boxed{29}

Think of the process as a sequence of 6 rolls R1,…,R6, where sticker i is placed on face Ri. A sticker i is visible at the end iff its roll Ri is the last time that face appears in the sequence.

The condition “all even-numbered stickers are visible” is equivalent to requiring that:

- 2 and 4 are visible, which means:
  - Rj ≠ R2 for j = 3,4,5,6,
  - Rj ≠ R4 for j = 5,6,
and in particular R2 ≠ R4 and R6 ≠ R2,R4.

Under this condition (call it E), the distributions simplify:
- R2 = v2 is uniform over 6 faces.
- R4 = v4 is uniform over the 5 faces ≠ v2.
- R3 is uniform over the 5 faces ≠ v2.
- R5 and R6 are independent uniform over the 4 faces ≠ v2, v4.
- R1 is uniform over all 6 faces, independent.

We want P(A | E), where A = “exactly one face is blank,” which is equivalent to “exactly one duplicate pair among the six rolls,” i.e., exactly one pair of equal indices and no other equalities.

Under E, the only possible duplicate pairs are:
(1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (1,5), (1,6), (3,4), (3,5), (3,6), (5,6).

Compute the probability for each pair to be the unique duplicate under the conditional distributions above:

- (1,2): R1 = v2 (prob 1/6); require R3 ≠ v4 (4/5); R5,R6 distinct and ≠ R3 (1/2). Total: 1/6·4/5·1/2 = 1/15.

- (1,3): R1 = R3 = x ∈ faces\{v2,v4} (prob 2/15); R5,R6 distinct and ≠ x (1/2). Total: 1/15.

- (1,4): R1 = v4 (1/6); require R3 ≠ v4 (4/5); R5,R6 distinct and ≠ R3 (1/2). Total: 1/15.

- (1,5): R1 = R5 = x ∈ faces\{v2,v4} (1/6); require R3 ∉ {x,v4} (3/5); R6 ∉ {x,R3} (1/2). Total: 1/20.

- (1,6): symmetric to (1,5). Total: 1/20.

- (3,4): R3 = v4 (1/5); require R1 ∉ {v2,v4} (4/6); R5,R6 distinct and ≠ R1 (1/2). Total: 1/15.

- (3,5): R3 = R5 = x ∈ faces\{v2,v4} (1/5); require R1 ∉ {v2,v4,x} (1/2); R6 ∉ {x,R1} (1/2). Total: 1/20.

- (3,6): symmetric to (3,5). Total: 1/20.

- (5,6): R5 = R6 = x ∈ faces\{v2,v4} (1/4); require R1 ∉ {v2,v4,x} (1/2); R3 ∉ {v4,x,R1} (2/5). Total: 1/20.

Summing these disjoint cases:
4 terms of 1/15 and 5 terms of 1/20 give
4/15 + 5/20 = 4/15 + 1/4 = (16 + 15)/60 = 31/60.

Therefore p = 31/60, so m + n = 31 + 60 = 91.

Final answer: boxed.

Think of the process as six independent fair die rolls, where roll i places sticker i on the top face. A face is blank at the end if it never appeared as the top face in any of the six rolls. Thus “exactly one blank face” means exactly five distinct faces were rolled among the six rolls.

Let E be the event “stickers 2, 4, and 6 are visible.” For a sticker k to be visible, its face must not appear again after time k. Hence:
- 6 is always visible.
- 2 is visible iff X2 ≠ X3, X4, X5, X6.
- 4 is visible iff X4 ≠ X5, X6.

Compute P(E):
- P(X3, X4, X5, X6 all ≠ X2) = (5/6)^4.
- Given that, X4, X5, X6 are uniform over 5 values and independent, so P(X5 ≠ X4 and X6 ≠ X4) = (4/5)^2.
Thus P(E) = (5/6)^4 · (4/5)^2 = 25/81.

Now let A be “exactly five distinct faces appear.” This means exactly one face appears twice and the other four appear once each. Let the duplicate occur at positions i<j. Then the only non-last occurrence index is i. Under A, E holds exactly when i ∈ {1,3,5} (so that 2 and 4 are last occurrences).

Count sequences with A and i ∈ {1,3,5}:
- Choose the repeated face: 6 ways.
- Choose j>i: (6−i) ways.
- Fill the other 4 positions with 4 distinct faces from the remaining 5: 5P4 = 120 ways.
For fixed i, this gives 6·(6−i)·120 sequences. Summing over i ∈ {1,3,5}:
Total = 6·120·[(6−1)+(6−3)+(6−5)] = 720·(5+3+1) = 6480.
Therefore P(A ∩ E) = 6480 / 6^6 = 6480 / 46656 = 5/36.

Thus the desired conditional probability is
p = P(A ∩ E) / P(E) = (5/36) / (25/81) = 9/20.

So m+n = 9+20 = 29.

Let X_k be the top face on roll k (k = 1,...,6). Each X_k is uniformly random over the 6 faces and independent. Sticker k is placed on face X_k, covering any previous stickers on that face. A sticker is visible at the end if no later sticker was placed on its face.

Step 2: Interpret the condition (all even-numbered stickers are visible)
Sticker 6 is always visible (it is last). For sticker 4 to be visible, its face must not appear at times 5 or 6. For sticker 2 to be visible, its face must not appear at times 3,4,5,6. Also, the faces at times 2, 4, 6 must be distinct (otherwise a later even sticker would cover an earlier one).

Let a = X_2, b = X_4, c = X_6. Then:
- a, b, c are distinct.
- For t = 3,4,5,6: X_t ≠ a.
- For t = 5,6: X_t ≠ b.
- X_6 = c is automatically fine.

Step 3: Count sequences satisfying the condition E (even stickers visible)
Choose ordered distinct faces (a,b,c): 6·5·4 = 120 ways.
For the remaining times:
- t=1: 6 options
- t=3: 5 options (not a)
- t=5: 4 options (not a or b)

Thus, number of sequences satisfying E:
N_E = 120 · (6 · 5 · 4) = 120 · 120 = 14400.

Step 4: Count those with exactly one face left blank
A face is blank if it never appears among X_1,...,X_6. Since a, b, c appear at times 2,4,6, any blank face must be among the remaining three faces R = F \ {a,b,c}. Exactly one blank means that among positions t=1,3,5, exactly two of the three faces in R appear at least once, and the third never appears.

Fix (a,b,c). Let S be a chosen 2-element subset of R (there are C(3,2)=3 choices). For the values at t=1,3,5, the allowed sets (to ensure no use of R \ S) are:
- t=1: {a,b,c} ∪ S (size 5)
- t=3: {b,c} ∪ S (size 4)
- t=5: {c} ∪ S (size 3)

We also require that both elements of S appear at least once among t=1,3,5. Counting by inclusion-exclusion:
- Total sequences: 5·4·3 = 60
- Minus those missing one chosen face (2 choices): 2 · (4·3·2) = 48
- Plus those missing both chosen faces: 3·2·1 = 6
Net = 60 − 48 + 6 = 18 per choice of S.

Thus, for fixed (a,b,c), the number with exactly one blank is 3 · 18 = 54. Therefore
N_{E,5} = 120 · 54 = 6480.

Step 5: Compute the conditional probability
p = N_{E,5} / N_E = 6480 / 14400 = 9/20.

So m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

Final answer: \boxed{29}

Step 1: Model the process as a sequence of six rolls X1, X2, ..., X6, where Xi is the face showing when sticker i is placed. A face is blank at the end iff it never appears among the Xi. Thus “exactly one face left blank” means exactly 5 distinct faces appear among X1..X6.

Step 2: The condition that all even-numbered stickers (2,4,6) are visible means:
- X2 is the last occurrence of its face: X2 ≠ X3, X4, X5, X6.
- X4 is the last occurrence of its face: X4 ≠ X5, X6.
- X6 is always last by virtue of being at time 6.
Also, X2, X4, X6 must be distinct.

Counting sequences satisfying this condition (call it B):
- Choices: X1: 6; X2: 6; X3: 5 (≠ X2); X4: 5 (≠ X2); X5: 4 (≠ X2, X4); X6: 4 (≠ X2, X4).
So total S_B = 6·6·5·5·4·4 = 14400.

Step 3: Let S_even = {X2, X4, X6} (three distinct faces). Let Y1 = X1, Y3 = X3, Y5 = X5. The total number of distinct faces among all six times equals 3 plus the number U of distinct faces among {Y1, Y3, Y5} that are not in S_even. Therefore, exactly one blank face (i.e., 5 distinct faces total) occurs iff U = 2.

Under B, the constraints on Y-positions are:
- Y1: any face (6 choices),
- Y3: any face except X2 (5 choices),
- Y5: any face except X2 or X4 (4 choices).

Step 4: For a fixed ordered triple (X2, X4, X6) of distinct faces, count the number of (Y1, Y3, Y5) with U = 2.

Let O be the set of the 3 faces not in S_even.

We need exactly two distinct faces from O to appear among (Y1, Y3, Y5). Two disjoint cases:

- Case A: Exactly two of {Y1, Y3, Y5} are in O and are distinct; the third is in S_even (subject to its position’s restriction).
  Number of ways:
  - If the S_even position is Y1: 6 (assign two distinct O faces to the other two positions) × 3 (choices for Y1 in S_even) = 18.
  - If S_even is Y3: 6 × 2 = 12.
  - If S_even is Y5: 6 × 1 = 6.
  Total Case A = 18 + 12 + 6 = 36.

- Case B: All three of Y1, Y3, Y5 are in O, using exactly two distinct O faces.
  Choose the two O faces: C(3,2) = 3; arrange them in length-3 sequence with both appearing: 2^3 − 2 = 6.
  Total Case B = 3 × 6 = 18.

Thus per ordered (X2, X4, X6), the number of valid (Y1, Y3, Y5) with U = 2 is 36 + 18 = 54. There are 6·5·4 = 120 ordered choices of (X2, X4, X6), so
S_{A∩B} = 120 × 54 = 6480.

Step 5: Therefore
p = P(A | B) = S_{A∩B} / S_B = 6480 / 14400 = 9/20.

Thus m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

Final answer: \boxed{29}

Let the sequence of top faces on the six rolls be F1, F2, ..., F6. After roll t we place sticker t on face Ft, covering any previous sticker there. A face is blank at the end iff it was never rolled; thus exactly one blank face means exactly five distinct faces appear among F1..F6.

Condition E: “All even-numbered stickers 2,4,6 are visible” translates to:
- F2 ≠ F3, F4, F5, F6
- F4 ≠ F5, F6
- F6 automatically visible but must differ from F2 and F4 so as not to cover them

Counting sequences satisfying E:
- F1: 6 choices
- F2: 6 choices
- F3: 5 choices (≠ F2)
- F4: 5 choices (≠ F2)
- F5: 4 choices (≠ F2, F4)
- F6: 4 choices (≠ F2, F4)

Total sequences in E: 6·6·5·5·4·4 = 14400.

Under E, the only allowed equalities among times (i.e., positions that may be equal without violating E) are:
12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 34, 35, 36, 56
(9 pairs). Any other equality would violate E.

Having exactly one blank face means exactly five distinct faces overall, i.e., exactly one pair of equal positions and all other positions distinct. For a fixed allowed pair (i, j):
- Choose the repeated face f*: 6 ways.
- Assign the other four positions injectively to four distinct faces from the remaining 5 faces: 5P4 = 120 ways.

Thus, per allowed pair: 6·120 = 720 sequences. With 9 allowed pairs:
Number in E with exactly one blank face = 9·720 = 6480.

Therefore, conditioned on E,
p = 6480 / 14400 = 9/20.

So m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

Final answer: \boxed{29}

Let the sequence of rolls be r1, r2, r3, r4, r5, r6, where ri is the face on which sticker i is placed.

Condition E (even stickers 2,4,6 visible) means:
- r6 can be any face.
- r4 must not be covered later: r4 ≠ r5, r6.
- r2 must not be covered later: r2 ≠ r3, r4, r5, r6.
Thus r2, r4, r6 are all distinct.

Counting sequences satisfying E:
- Choose (r2, r4, r6) as distinct faces: 6·5·4 ways.
- Choose r3 ∈ faces ≠ r2: 5 ways.
- Choose r5 ∈ faces ≠ r2, r4: 4 ways.
- Choose r1 freely: 6 ways.
Total count(E) = 6·5·4·5·4·6 = 14400.

Event B (exactly one face left blank) means exactly five distinct faces appear among r1,…,r6. Since r2, r4, r6 already use three distinct faces, this happens iff among {r1, r3, r5} exactly two of the three faces not in {r2, r4, r6} appear.

Fix r2=a, r4=b, r6=c. For (r1, r3, r5), the allowed domains under E are:
- r1: any of 6 faces.
- r3: any of 5 faces (≠ a).
- r5: any of 4 faces (≠ a, b).
Let T={a,b,c} and D be the other 3 faces. We need exactly two distinct faces from D to appear among r1, r3, r5.

For a fixed choice of two faces U⊂D, |U|=2, and excluding the third w∈D:
- The per-position allowed sizes (T∪U) are 5, 4, 3, so total 60.
- Subtract those with no outside face: 3·2·1 = 6.
- Subtract those with only one of U: for each u∈U, (T∪{u}) gives 4·3·2=24; minus all-in-T (6) gives 18. For two choices, subtract 36.
Thus for this U, count = 60 − 6 − 36 = 18. With 3 choices of U, total 54.

Hence, for each (a,b,c), there are 54 suitable triples (r1, r3, r5). Therefore
count(E ∩ B) = (6·5·4)·54 = 6480.

So p = count(E ∩ B)/count(E) = 6480/14400 = 9/20, and m+n = 9+20 = 29.

Final answer: \boxed{29}

Let X1, X2, ..., X6 be the (independent uniform) top faces obtained on the six rolls. A sticker k is visible at the end if and only if its face is not rolled again after time k. Thus the condition that all even stickers 2, 4, 6 are visible is equivalent to:
- X2 ∉ {X3, X4, X5, X6}
- X4 ∉ {X5, X6}
- X6 is always visible.

We will count sequences satisfying these constraints, and among them those with exactly one blank face (i.e., exactly 5 distinct faces used, which is equivalent to exactly one duplicate pair among X1,...,X6).

Counting sequences with even stickers visible:
- X1 is free: 6 choices.
- For (X2,...,X6), split by whether X5 = X6 (case B) or X5 ≠ X6 (case A).

Case A: X5 ≠ X6.
- Choose (X5, X6): 6·5 = 30.
- Choose X4 ≠ X5, X6: 4 choices.
- Let s = |{X4, X5, X6}| = 3. For each fixed triple, the number of (X3, X2) pairs satisfying X2 ∉ {X3, X4, X5, X6} equals 5(6 − s) = 15.
Thus total for (X2,...,X6): 30·4·15 = 1800.

Case B: X5 = X6.
- Choose X5 = X6: 6 choices.
- Choose X4 ≠ X5: 5 choices.
- Here s = 2, so number of (X3, X2) pairs = 5(6 − s) = 20.
Thus total: 6·5·20 = 600.

Therefore total sequences with even stickers visible:
N(V) = 6 · (1800 + 600) = 14400.

Now count those with exactly one blank face (i.e., exactly one duplicate pair among X1..X6) under the same constraints.

Case A (X5 ≠ X6, X4 distinct from both): For each fixed triple (X5, X6, X4), the total number of (X3, X2) under the visibility constraints is 15; with X1 free, that’s 90 possibilities. The only allowed single-duplicate pairs (consistent with the visibility constraints) are:
(1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (1,5), (1,6), (3,4), (3,5), (3,6).
Each such pair contributes 6 valid assignments of (X3, X2, X1), so 8·6 = 48 favorable per triple.
Number of triples: 30·4 = 120.
Thus N_A = 120 · 48 = 5760.

Case B (X5 = X6, X4 ≠ X5): The only permissible duplicate is (5,6). Then X1, X2, X3 must be distinct from each other and from X4 and X5. From the remaining 4 values, choose an ordered triple for (X3, X2, X1): 4·3·2 = 24 per base (X5, X4).
There are 6·5 = 30 such base choices.
Thus N_B = 30 · 24 = 720.

Therefore N(B ∩ V) = 5760 + 720 = 6480.

Hence the desired conditional probability is
p = N(B ∩ V) / N(V) = 6480 / 14400 = 9/20.

So m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

Final answer: \boxed{29}
